title.en: Azure OpenAI Service: Enterprise LLMs Done Right

description.en: Run frontier models inside enterprise guardrails: Azure OpenAI deployments and quota, TPM rate limits and 429 handling, provisioned vs pay-as-you-go throughput, content filtering, networking, and RAG grounding.

story_variants.en:
## Why Enterprises Route LLMs Through Azure

The models in **Azure OpenAI Service** (increasingly surfaced through the broader **Azure AI Foundry** portal alongside model catalogs from other providers) are the draw, but the *reason* enterprises adopt it is everything wrapped around them: the service runs inside your subscription's governance perimeter. Your prompts and completions are **not used to train the underlying models**; data stays within the service boundary and your chosen geography; access is Entra-controlled; and traffic can be forced over **private endpoints** so nothing touches the public internet. In other words: the whole track so far — identity, networking, policy — applies verbatim to AI.

## Deployments: The Unit You Actually Manage

You create an Azure OpenAI *resource*, then **deployments** inside it: a deployment binds a model + version (e.g., a GPT-4-class chat model or an embeddings model) to a name your applications call. This indirection is operational gold — you upgrade a model version by cutting a new deployment and repointing, and you pin versions so an upstream model retirement never surprises production. Watch model **retirement dates**: they are published and enforceable, so version pinning plus an upgrade calendar is a real operational duty.

Deployment types matter:

- **Standard** — pay-as-you-go per token, regional.
- **Global Standard / Data Zone** — pay-as-you-go with routing across Microsoft's global or geography-bounded capacity for better availability; data-at-rest and processing boundaries are documented per type — regulated industries read that page closely.
- **Provisioned (PTU)** — reserved throughput units for predictable latency at scale; the pricing model shifts from per-token to per-provisioned-unit, with commitments discounting further.

## The Rate-Limit Physics: TPM, RPM, 429

Quota is allocated as **tokens-per-minute (TPM)** per model per region per subscription, which you split across deployments; a proportional requests-per-minute (RPM) limit rides along. Exceed either and the API returns **429 with a Retry-After header**.

Enterprise-grade clients therefore always implement:

1. **Exponential backoff with jitter**, honoring Retry-After.
2. **Client-side budgeting** — count tokens *before* sending (`tiktoken`-style tokenizers) and keep prompts lean.
3. **Spillover routing** — a gateway (commonly **Azure API Management** with its GenAI/token-limit policies) that load-balances across deployments in multiple regions and can enforce per-team token quotas and chargeback.

> **War story:** A support-bot team load-tested at 50 concurrent chats, shipped, and fell over at 300 when every retry storm amplified the original overload — classic thundering herd, because their retry had no jitter and ignored Retry-After. The fix was three lines of backoff discipline plus an APIM token-limit policy; the postmortem's title was 'We DDoSed ourselves politely'.

## Safety and Grounding

- **Content filtering** runs on prompts and completions across harm categories (hate, sexual, violence, self-harm, plus jailbreak/prompt-injection shields) with configurable severity thresholds; annotations tell you what tripped. Modifying filters beyond defaults is a gated request.
- **RAG (retrieval-augmented generation)** is the enterprise default for grounding: embed your documents (an embeddings deployment), index them in **Azure AI Search** (vector + hybrid + semantic ranking), retrieve top-k chunks per query, and stuff them into the prompt with citations. The 'On Your Data' feature packages this pattern; serious teams eventually own the pipeline for chunking and evaluation control.
- **Evaluation and observability**: log token usage per request (it is in every response), trace prompts (with PII discipline), and build golden-set evals before you need them. Azure AI Foundry ships evaluation tooling; the observability lesson's habits apply to LLMs with extra teeth.

| Concern | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| AuthN/AuthZ | Entra ID + RBAC (Cognitive Services OpenAI User), managed identities, disable local keys |
| Network | Private endpoints, deny public access, APIM gateway in front |
| Cost control | TPM splits per deployment, APIM token quotas, PTU for baseline + standard for burst |
| Safety | Content filters, jailbreak shields, abuse monitoring |
| Data boundary | No training on your data; geography per deployment type |

## Cost Model Thinking

Token pricing differs by model family and direction (input vs output tokens — output usually costs more). The enterprise pattern mirrors compute: **PTU reservations for the steady baseline, pay-as-you-go standard deployments for burst**, and a gateway that routes accordingly. Cheap wins first: trim system prompts, cache common answers, use a smaller model for triage and escalate to the frontier model only when needed — a 'model ladder' that routinely cuts spend dramatically with negligible quality loss.

## Common Pitfalls

- Calling the API with keys stored in config instead of managed identity + RBAC.
- No version pinning — a silent auto-upgrade changes behavior mid-quarter.
- Ignoring Retry-After and retrying instantly — self-inflicted DDoS.
- Sending the entire document instead of retrieved chunks — token bills and context-window failures.
- Skipping content-filter annotation handling, so users see raw 400s when a filter trips.
- Treating evals as optional — you cannot safely change prompts, models, or filters without a regression suite.

## Mental Model

Treat the LLM as **a rate-limited, token-metered dependency with a safety interlock** — architecturally closer to a payments API than to a database. Everything you already know applies: identity instead of keys, private networking, quotas and backoff, version pinning, canary evals. The magic is in the model; the *engineering* is the same discipline this track has been teaching all along.

description.en: The enterprise LLM call path: gateway-fronted, quota-metered, content-filtered, grounded by retrieval, private end to end.

left.label: Instant retries on 429, no jitter — the retry storm amplifies the overload

right.label: Exponential backoff with jitter, honoring Retry-After

description.en: 429 is the service telling you when to come back; polite clients read the header instead of stampeding.

animation_sequence.code:
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential, get_bearer_token_provider
from openai import AzureOpenAI
token = get_bearer_token_provider(DefaultAzureCredential(),
    'https://cognitiveservices.azure.com/.default')
client = AzureOpenAI(azure_endpoint=ep, azure_ad_token_provider=token,
    api_version='2024-10-21')
r = client.chat.completions.create(model='chat-prod',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': q}])
print(r.usage.total_tokens)

explanation.en: Entra token instead of API keys — managed identity discipline extends to AI endpoints; disable local keys by policy.
'chat-prod' is your deployment name, pinning a model version your team upgrades deliberately.
Every response reports token usage — log it per request; it is your cost meter and your quota telemetry.

lines: operation have exceeded token rate limit..."}}

description.en: The TPM ceiling in the wild: 429 plus Retry-After — the two fields every production client must respect.
One grounded RAG round-trip, from question to cited, filtered, metered answer.

instruction.en: Simulate an Azure OpenAI TPM rate limiter. Quota is TPM_QUOTA = 10000 tokens per fixed minute window (window id = timestamp // 60). Implement a RateLimiter class with allow(ts, tokens): if the request's window differs from the current one, reset usage; accept when used + tokens <= quota (add to usage, return True), else return False (a 429 — usage unchanged). Process REQUESTS in order printing 't=<ts> tokens=<tokens> -> <200 or 429>' per request, then 'accepted=<n> rejected=<n>'. This is the exact fixed-window accounting behind those retry-after headers.

practice_challenges.expected_output:
t=0 tokens=4000 -> 200
t=10 tokens=5000 -> 200
t=20 tokens=2000 -> 429
t=30 tokens=1000 -> 200
t=65 tokens=8000 -> 200
t=70 tokens=3000 -> 429
accepted=4 rejected=2

en: Fixed window: window = ts // 60. Requests at t=0..30 share window 0; t=65 and t=70 are window 1 with fresh usage.
The t=20 request (2000 tokens) is rejected because 9000+2000 exceeds 10000 — but t=30's 1000 tokens still fits exactly (10000 <= 10000).
A rejected request must NOT consume tokens: only add to usage after the acceptance check passes.

practice_challenges.test_code:
rl = RateLimiter(10000)
assert rl.allow(0, 4000) == True
assert rl.allow(10, 5000) == True
assert rl.allow(20, 2000) == False
assert rl.allow(30, 1000) == True
assert rl.allow(65, 8000) == True
assert rl.allow(70, 3000) == False
rl2 = RateLimiter(100)
assert rl2.allow(0, 100) == True
assert rl2.allow(1, 1) == False
assert rl2.allow(60, 1) == True

en: A resource group containing the model
A named binding of a model + version that applications call — enabling version pinning and controlled upgrades
A Kubernetes pod running the model weights
A billing export of token usage

q.en: When the service returns 429, a well-behaved client should...

en: Retry immediately in a tight loop
Back off exponentially with jitter and honor the Retry-After header

q.en: Provisioned throughput (PTU) deployments change the economics how?

en: Reserved throughput billed per provisioned unit — predictable latency for steady baselines, vs per-token pay-as-you-go for burst

q.en: Does Azure OpenAI use your prompts and completions to train the underlying foundation models?

en: Yes, unless you opt out per request
No — customer data is not used to train the underlying models, and stays within the service boundary and chosen geography
